---
title: "Privileged Basis - How does ReLU cause sparsity and ecnourage privileged basis?"
author: "Ishaan Shrivastava"
---


---
title: "Prerequisite Study Guide: ReLU Privileged-Basis Reconstruction"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
---

# Purpose

The target object is

$$
L(Q)
=
\inf_g
\mathbb E\!\left[
\left\|Z-g(\operatorname{ReLU}(QZ))\right\|^2
\right].
$$

Your existing notes already cover the algebraic prerequisites:

- expectation,
- covariance,
- independence,
- finite second moments,
- norms, traces, and outer products.

This guide covers only the remaining load-bearing material:

1. measurable spaces, measurable maps, generated sigma-algebras, and laws;
2. conditional expectation, tower property, and conditional variance;
3. conditional expectation as an orthogonal projection in \(L^2\), hence the MMSE theorem.

# Primary reading

Use:

**Nima Moshayedi, _Lectures on Probability Theory_, University of Zurich**

<https://arxiv.org/pdf/2010.16280>

The page numbers below refer to the printed page numbers in the notes.

## Level 1 — Information and measurability

Read only:

- Appendix A, §1, **Measurable Spaces**, pp. 143–144.
- Appendix A, §3, **Borel Sets**, pp. 146–147.
- Appendix A, §5, **Measurable Maps**, pp. 151–152.
- Chapter 2, §1 and §1.1, **General Definitions** and **Law of a Random Variable**, pp. 25–28.
- Chapter 2, §2.4 and Proposition 2.2, **Sigma-algebras generated by a random variable**, pp. 33–34.

You should leave this level knowing precisely:

$$
X:(\Omega,\mathcal F)\to(E,\mathcal E)
$$

is measurable iff

$$
X^{-1}(B)\in\mathcal F
\qquad
\text{for every }B\in\mathcal E,
$$

and

$$
\sigma(X)
=
\{X^{-1}(B):B\in\mathcal E\}.
$$

The crucial factorization theorem is:

$$
Y\text{ is }\sigma(X)\text{-measurable}
\iff
Y=f(X)
$$

for some measurable \(f\), in the setting used here.

## Level 2 — Conditional expectation

Read only:

- Chapter 3, §2.2, **Discrete construction**, pp. 80–82.
- Chapter 3, §2.3 through Theorem 2.18, **General construction and basic properties**, pp. 82–84.
- Theorem 2.29, **Tower property**, p. 86.

Skip on the first pass:

- the Radon–Nikodym construction;
- transition kernels;
- martingales.

You should leave this level knowing that

$$
\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]
$$

is a \(\mathcal G\)-measurable random variable satisfying

$$
\mathbb E[X1_G]
=
\mathbb E[\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]1_G]
\qquad
\text{for every }G\in\mathcal G,
$$

and that, for \(\mathcal H\subseteq\mathcal G\),

$$
\mathbb E[\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]\mid\mathcal H]
=
\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal H].
$$

## Level 3 — \(L^2\) geometry and MMSE

Read only:

- Chapter 3, §1, **\(L^2(\Omega,\mathcal F,\mathbb P)\) as a Hilbert space**, pp. 73–75.
- Chapter 3, §1.2, **Orthogonal projection**, pp. 78–79.
- Definitions 2.12 and 2.15 and Remarks 2.13–2.14, pp. 82–83.
- The first two paragraphs of §4.1.2, pp. 90–91, emphasizing the distinction between:
  - projection onto all \(\sigma(A)\)-measurable functions;
  - projection onto the much smaller linear span of the observed variables.

You should leave this level able to prove

$$
\mathbb E\|Z-g(A)\|^2
=
\mathbb E\|Z-\mathbb E[Z\mid A]\|^2
+
\mathbb E\|\mathbb E[Z\mid A]-g(A)\|^2.
$$

This identity is the actual reason conditional expectation is the MMSE decoder.

# Optional terse reference

For a compact formal reference:

**Matija Vidmar, _Probability with Measure_**

<https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.01685>

Relevant sections:

- §1.1, measurable sets;
- §1.3, measurable maps and generated sigma-fields;
- §3.3, conditioning.

Do not use this as the first exposition unless you prefer definition-dense notes.

# How to use the quiz

Do three passes.

1. **Closed-notes pass:** attempt every question without reading the answer key.
2. **Open-notes repair pass:** repair incomplete definitions and proofs.
3. **Compression pass:** rewrite each starred proof in at most ten lines without omitting assumptions.

Each ordinary question is worth \(2\) points:

- \(0\): absent, materially false, or circular;
- \(1\): right idea but missing a required definition, assumption, or proof step;
- \(2\): correct and explicit.

A starred question is load-bearing. A zero on any starred question means the corresponding level is not yet secure, regardless of total score.

# Quiz

# Part I — Measurability and information

## Question 1 ★ — Sigma-algebra

State the definition of a sigma-algebra \(\mathcal F\) on a set \(\Omega\).

Then state the definition of a probability space

$$
(\Omega,\mathcal F,\mathbb P).
$$

Do not explain by analogy. Give the axioms.

## Question 2 — Finite examples

Let

$$
\Omega=\{1,2,3,4\}
$$

and

$$
\mathcal G
=
\{\varnothing,\Omega,\{1,2\},\{3,4\}\}.
$$

1. Prove that \(\mathcal G\) is a sigma-algebra.
2. Let
   $$
   \mathcal H
   =
   \mathcal G\cup\{\{1,3\},\{2,4\}\}.
   $$
   Is \(\mathcal H\) a sigma-algebra? Prove your answer.

## Question 3 ★ — Measurable map

Let \((\Omega,\mathcal F)\) and \((E,\mathcal E)\) be measurable spaces.

State precisely what it means for

$$
X:\Omega\to E
$$

to be measurable.

Why is the definition written using **preimages**, not images?

## Question 4 — Composition

Let

$$
X:(\Omega,\mathcal F)\to(E,\mathcal E)
$$

and

$$
f:(E,\mathcal E)\to(S,\mathcal S)
$$

be measurable.

Prove directly from the definition that \(f\circ X\) is measurable.

## Question 5 ★ — Generated sigma-algebra

Let

$$
\Omega=\{\omega_1,\omega_2,\omega_3,\omega_4\}
$$

and define

$$
X(\omega_1)=0,\qquad
X(\omega_2)=0,\qquad
X(\omega_3)=1,\qquad
X(\omega_4)=2.
$$

Assume the codomain has the full power-set sigma-algebra.

List every element of \(\sigma(X)\).

Interpret \(\sigma(X)\) as the information revealed by observing \(X\).

## Question 6 — Law or pushforward distribution

Suppose

$$
\mathbb P(\{\omega_1\})=0.1,\quad
\mathbb P(\{\omega_2\})=0.2,\quad
\mathbb P(\{\omega_3\})=0.3,\quad
\mathbb P(\{\omega_4\})=0.4.
$$

Using the \(X\) from Question 5, compute the law

$$
\mathbb P_X
=
\mathbb P\circ X^{-1}.
$$

Give its value on every subset of \(\{0,1,2\}\).

## Question 7 ★ — Factorization through the observation

State the factorization theorem relating these two statements:

1. \(Y\) is \(\sigma(X)\)-measurable.
2. \(Y=f(X)\) for some measurable function \(f\).

Use it to explain rigorously why an estimator that is allowed to use only \(A\) has the form

$$
g(A).
$$

## Question 8 — Type checking

For a random vector \(A:\Omega\to\mathbb R^d\), distinguish the types of:

1. \(A\);
2. \(a\in\mathbb R^d\);
3. \(\sigma(A)\);
4. \(\{A\in B\}\), where \(B\in\mathcal B(\mathbb R^d)\);
5. \(\mathbb E[Z\mid A]\).

## Question 9 ★ — Measurability of the ReLU observation

Assume

$$
Z:(\Omega,\mathcal F)\to(\mathbb R^d,\mathcal B(\mathbb R^d))
$$

is measurable and \(Q\in\mathbb R^{d\times d}\) is fixed.

Prove that

$$
A=\operatorname{ReLU}(QZ)
$$

is measurable.

State every closure fact you use.

# Part II — Conditional expectation

## Question 10 ★ — Definition

Let \(X\in L^1\) and let \(\mathcal G\subseteq\mathcal F\) be a sub-sigma-algebra.

State the defining properties of

$$
Y=\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G].
$$

Include the sense in which \(Y\) is unique.

## Question 11 — Finite partition calculation

Let

$$
\Omega=\{1,2,3,4\},
$$

with probabilities

$$
(0.1,0.2,0.3,0.4),
$$

and let

$$
X=(2,-1,4,0)
$$

on these four outcomes.

Let

$$
\mathcal G
=
\sigma(\{1,2\},\{3,4\}).
$$

Compute \(\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]\) as a random variable on \(\Omega\).

## Question 12 ★ — Verify from the definition

For your answer to Question 11, verify explicitly that

$$
\mathbb E[X1_G]
=
\mathbb E[\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]1_G]
$$

for every \(G\in\mathcal G\).

## Question 13 — Already known information

Prove:

If \(X\) is \(\mathcal G\)-measurable and integrable, then

$$
\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]=X
\qquad\text{a.s.}
$$

## Question 14 — Irrelevant information

Prove:

If \(X\) is integrable and independent of \(\mathcal G\), then

$$
\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]
=
\mathbb E[X]
\qquad\text{a.s.}
$$

## Question 15 ★ — Tower property

Let

$$
\mathcal H\subseteq\mathcal G\subseteq\mathcal F
$$

and \(X\in L^1\).

State and prove

$$
\mathbb E[\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]\mid\mathcal H]
=
\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal H].
$$

Use the defining property of conditional expectation, not an informal averaging argument.

## Question 16 ★ — Law of total variance

For \(X\in L^2\), prove

$$
\operatorname{Var}(X)
=
\mathbb E[\operatorname{Var}(X\mid\mathcal G)]
+
\operatorname{Var}(\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]).
$$

Define

$$
\operatorname{Var}(X\mid\mathcal G)
=
\mathbb E[(X-\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G])^2\mid\mathcal G].
$$

Identify exactly where the cross term vanishes.

## Question 17 — Vector version

For \(Z\in L^2(\Omega;\mathbb R^d)\), prove

$$
\operatorname{Cov}(Z)
=
\mathbb E[\operatorname{Cov}(Z\mid\mathcal G)]
+
\operatorname{Cov}(\mathbb E[Z\mid\mathcal G]).
$$

All quantities are matrices. State what expectation of a random matrix means.

## Question 18 ★ — Trace of conditional covariance

Let

$$
m=\mathbb E[Z\mid\mathcal G].
$$

Prove

$$
\operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{Cov}(Z\mid\mathcal G))
=
\mathbb E[\|Z-m\|^2\mid\mathcal G].
$$

Then prove

$$
\mathbb E[
\operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{Cov}(Z\mid\mathcal G))
]
=
\mathbb E\|Z-m\|^2.
$$

Do not remove the conditional expectation in the first identity.

## Question 19 — Scalar ReLU with a uniform latent

Let

$$
Z\sim\operatorname{Unif}[-1,1],
\qquad
A=\operatorname{ReLU}(Z).
$$

Compute explicitly:

1. \(\mathbb E[Z\mid A]\) as a function \(h(A)\);
2. \(\operatorname{Var}(Z\mid A)\);
3. the MMSE
   $$
   \mathbb E[(Z-\mathbb E[Z\mid A])^2].
   $$

## Question 20 — Scalar Gaussian ReLU

Let

$$
Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1),
\qquad
A=\operatorname{ReLU}(Z).
$$

You may use

$$
\mathbb E[Z\mid Z\le 0]
=
-\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}},
\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(Z\mid Z\le 0)
=
1-\frac{2}{\pi}.
$$

Compute \(\mathbb E[Z\mid A]\) and the MMSE.

## Question 21 — Conditioning on a continuously distributed observation

Explain why the notation

$$
\mathbb E[Z\mid A=a]
$$

cannot generally be defined by the elementary formula

$$
\frac{\mathbb E[Z1_{\{A=a\}}]}{\mathbb P(A=a)}
$$

when \(A\) is continuous.

Explain why

$$
\mathbb E[Z\mid A]
=
\mathbb E[Z\mid\sigma(A)]
$$

remains well-defined.

## Question 22 — Sparse nonnegative scalar

Let

$$
Z=
\begin{cases}
0,&\text{with probability }1-p,\\
X,&\text{with probability }p,
\end{cases}
\qquad
X\sim\operatorname{Exp}(1),
$$

and let \(A=\operatorname{ReLU}(Z)\).

Compute \(\mathbb E[Z\mid A]\) and the MMSE.

# Part III — \(L^2\) geometry and MMSE

## Question 23 ★ — \(L^2\) as an inner-product space

Define

$$
L^2(\Omega,\mathcal F,\mathbb P)
$$

and its inner product and norm.

Why are random variables that are equal almost surely treated as the same element of \(L^2\)?

## Question 24 — The observable subspace

For a sub-sigma-algebra \(\mathcal G\), define

$$
L^2(\Omega,\mathcal G,\mathbb P).
$$

Prove it is a linear subspace of \(L^2(\Omega,\mathcal F,\mathbb P)\).

State the additional property needed before the orthogonal projection theorem applies.

## Question 25 ★ — Projection theorem

State the orthogonal projection theorem for a closed linear subspace \(M\) of a Hilbert space \(H\).

Give both equivalent characterizations of the projection \(P_Mx\):

1. closest point;
2. orthogonality of the residual.

## Question 26 ★ — Conditional expectation is the projection

Let \(X\in L^2\).

Prove that

$$
m=\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]
$$

satisfies

$$
\mathbb E[(X-m)V]=0
$$

for every \(V\in L^2(\Omega,\mathcal G,\mathbb P)\).

First prove it for indicator functions, then simple functions, then general \(V\).

## Question 27 ★ — Pythagorean risk decomposition

Let

$$
Z\in L^2(\Omega;\mathbb R^d),
\qquad
m=\mathbb E[Z\mid\mathcal G],
$$

and let \(T\) be any square-integrable \(\mathcal G\)-measurable random vector.

Prove

$$
\mathbb E\|Z-T\|^2
=
\mathbb E\|Z-m\|^2
+
\mathbb E\|m-T\|^2.
$$

Expand the square and prove that the cross term is exactly zero.

## Question 28 ★ — MMSE decoder

Set

$$
A=\operatorname{ReLU}(QZ).
$$

Using Questions 7 and 27, prove

$$
\inf_g
\mathbb E\|Z-g(A)\|^2
=
\mathbb E\|Z-\mathbb E[Z\mid A]\|^2.
$$

State:

1. the admissible class of \(g\);
2. the integrability condition;
3. the sense in which the minimizer is unique.

## Question 29 ★ — Conditional covariance formula for \(L(Q)\)

Prove

$$
L(Q)
=
\mathbb E[
\operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{Cov}(Z\mid A))
],
\qquad
A=\operatorname{ReLU}(QZ).
$$

Your proof should be a chain of explicit equalities.

## Question 30 ★ — Exact recoverability

Prove the equivalence

$$
L(Q)=0
$$

if and only if

$$
Z\text{ is }\sigma(A)\text{-measurable},
$$

if and only if there exists a measurable \(g\) such that

$$
Z=g(A)
\qquad\text{a.s.}
$$

Explain why this is an **almost-sure recoverability** statement, not necessarily a statement that the deterministic map

$$
z\mapsto\operatorname{ReLU}(Qz)
$$

is globally injective on all of \(\mathbb R^d\).

## Question 31 ★ — Arbitrary decoder versus linear decoder

Explain the difference between minimizing over:

$$
g(A)\quad\text{for every measurable }g
$$

and minimizing over:

$$
BA+b.
$$

Give a concrete example in which the MMSE decoder is nonlinear and therefore cannot be represented by an affine decoder.

## Question 32 ★ — Gaussian rotational invariance

Assume

$$
Z\sim\mathcal N(0,I_d)
$$

and \(Q\in O(d)\).

Prove that \(L(Q)\) is independent of \(Q\).

Your proof must use:

1. \(Y=QZ\sim\mathcal N(0,I_d)\);
2. preservation of Euclidean norm by \(Q\);
3. a bijection between decoders for \(Z\) and decoders for \(Y\).

# Bridge problems toward the privileged-basis proof

These are not prerequisites. They test whether you can now begin the actual problem.

## Bridge 1 — Identity basis for nonnegative latents

Assume

$$
Z\in[0,\infty)^d
\qquad\text{a.s.}
$$

Prove

$$
L(I)=0.
$$

The sparsity and exponential assumptions are irrelevant for this step. Explain why.

## Bridge 2 — Why one collision is insufficient

Someone argues:

> I found \(z\neq z'\) such that
> \[
> \operatorname{ReLU}(Qz)
> =
> \operatorname{ReLU}(Qz').
> \]
> Therefore \(L(Q)>0\).

Give at least three reasons this argument is incomplete.

## Bridge 3 — Correct positivity criterion

State a rigorous condition, in terms of conditional covariance, sufficient and necessary for

$$
L(Q)>0.
$$

Then state a more concrete sufficient condition involving a positive-probability set of observations.

## Bridge 4 — Forty-five-degree sparse case

Let \(d=2\) and

$$
Q
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt 2}
\begin{bmatrix}
1&-1\\
1&1
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

Let \(Z_1,Z_2\) be independent sparse nonnegative exponential variables.

Write

$$
A=\operatorname{ReLU}(QZ)
$$

coordinatewise.

Identify a region of latent space on which one ReLU coordinate is zero and the other reveals only \(Z_1+Z_2\).

Formulate a rigorous route to proving that the conditional distribution of \(Z\) on this region has nonzero variance. You do not need to complete all measure-theoretic details, but you must identify exactly what must be proved.

# Readiness criterion

There are \(32\) main questions, for \(64\) points.

You are ready to begin the privileged-basis proof when:

- total score is at least \(52/64\);
- no starred question receives \(0\);
- Questions 27–30 can be reproduced without notes;
- you can explain in one paragraph why the problem is fundamentally about the conditional spread of ReLU fibres under the law of \(Z\).

The answer key is a separate file. Do not open it until completing the closed-notes pass.
